coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen–doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent nanosensor for selective determination and cellular imaging of ClO−
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Zheng
Xu, Tiantian
Zhang, Xu
Li, Hui
Jia, Xiaodan
Zhao, Shunsheng
Yang, Zaiwen
Liu, Xiangrong
coredata.dc:description: The carbon nanomaterial based fluorescent probes have been widely applied in biological imaging. In the current research, we propose an interesting strategy for selective sensing of hypochlorite (ClO−) by a water–soluble and highly fluorescent nanosensor based on the N–doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) which was fabricated by a facile and environmental friendly hydrothermal approach from polyvinyl pyrrolidone, L–arginine and tryptophan. The structural characteristics of the probe were measured by multitudinous methods which proved the nanometer spherical structure of the probe and the successfully N–doping. Fluorescent investigation demonstrated that the probe is not only highly stable under interferences of pH, ionic strength, and irradiation, but also significantly selective toward ClO− amongst a variety of attractive bioactive species through the fluorescent quenching process which was correlative with the concentration of ClO− and linearly in the range of 0.1–50 μmol·L–1 with the sensitivity of 0.03 μmol·L–1. The probe can also be further illustrated in a prospective application for determination of ClO− in environmental water through both solution response and filer paper sensing. Moreover, the positive biocompatibility and ignorable cytotoxicity made the probe a promising effective agent for detection and visualizing ClO− in living cells which can facilitate the understanding the oxidative stress from the overexpressing ClO−.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent probes
Hypochlorite
Environmental water analysis
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental method of filter paper sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological and structural characterization of Y1. (a) Large–scale TEM image in 20 nm. (b) TEM image of Y1 in 10 nm. (c) High–resolution TEM (HR–TEM) image of Y1. (d) Size distribution of Y1. (e) XRD spectrum. (f) Raman spectrum.
(a) FT–IR spectrum of Y1. (b) Fluorescence decay curve of Y1. (c) XPS full spectrum of Y1. (d) XPS C 1 s spectrum of Y1. (e) XPS O 1 s spectrum of Y1. (f) XPS N 1 s spectrum of Y1.

Photophysical characterizations of Y1. (a) UV–vis, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. Inset: Photos of the CDs solution under daylight and UV light at 365 nm. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Y1 solution subjected to the varying excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm. (c) Selectivity and competivity investigation of Y1. Pink bars represent the fluorescence response of Y1 (1 mg·L–1) to 50 μmol·L–1 of the analytes. 1: blank, 2: Ag+, 3: Sn2+, 4: Ca2+, 5: Mg2+, 6: Cd2+, 7: Mn2+, 8: Co2+, 9: Ni2+, 10: Zn2+, 11: Pb2+, 12: Hg2+, 13: Cu2+, 14: Fe3+, 15: Cr3+, 16: Al3+, 17: Sn4+, 18: AMP, 19: ADP, 20: ATP, 21: Cys, 22: GSH, 23: Hcy, 24: O2 ·–, 25: H2O2, 26: NO, 27: S2–, 28: ONOO–, 29: NO3 2–, 30: PO4 3–, 31: CO3 2–, 32: OAc–, 33: C2O4 2–, 34: SO3 2–, 35: AA, 36: DA, 37: ClO4 –, 38: ClO3 –, 39: ClO2 –, 40: ClO–. Green bars represent the fluorescence response with the subsequent addition of 50 μmol·L–1 of ClO– to the above solutions. (d) pH investigation of Y1 (1 mg·L–1) in the presence of 50 μmol·L–1 of ClO– in aqueous solution with different pH conditions. (e) Fluorescence titration of Y1 (1 mg·L–1) upon addition of ClO– (0–50 μmol·L–1) in aqueous solution. (f) Linear relationship of the fluorescence intensity of Y1 as a function of ClO– concentration at 439 nm. λex = 350 nm.

(a) Absorption spectra of Y1 and Y1 + ClO–. (b) FT–IR spectra of Y1 and Y1 + ClO–. (c) 1H NMR spectra of Y1 and Y1 + ClO–. (d) Fluorescence decay curves of Y1 and Y1 + ClO–. (e) XPS full spectrum of Y1 + ClO–. (f) XPS C 1 s spectrum of Y1 + ClO–. (g) XPS N 1 s spectrum of Y1 + ClO–. (h) XPS O 1 s spectrum of Y1 + ClO–. (i) XPS Cl 2p spectrum of Y1 + ClO–. (j) Proposed mechanism of the probe with ClO–.
Filter paper sensing based on Y1 under UV light at 365 nm: (a) various ions and bioactive molecular, (b) practical water samples in containing of ClO–.

Fluorescent images of Human Osteosarcoma MG–63 cells (a1) and Mouse fibroblast cells L929 (a4). Pages of the cells incubated with Y1 (1 mg·L–1) for 30 min at 37 °C (a2: MG–63 cells, a5: L929 cells). The cells then further incubated with 50 μmol·L–1 of NaClO for another 30 min at 37 °C and imaged (a3: MG–63 cells, a6: L929 cells). (b). Brightfield transmission image of the cells. (c). Overlay images of (a) and (b).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen–doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent nanosensor for selective determination and cellular imaging of ClO−

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Xi’an University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon nanomaterial based fluorescent probes have been widely applied in biological imaging. In the current research, we propose an interesting strategy for selective sensing of hypochlorite (ClO−) by a water–soluble and highly fluorescent nanosensor based on the N–doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) which was fabricated by a facile and environmental friendly hydrothermal approach from polyvinyl pyrrolidone, L–arginine and tryptophan. The structural characteristics of the probe were measured by multitudinous methods which proved the nanometer spherical structure of the probe and the successfully N–doping. Fluorescent investigation demonstrated that the probe is not only highly stable under interferences of pH, ionic strength, and irradiation, but also significantly selective toward ClO− amongst a variety of attractive bioactive species through the fluorescent quenching process which was correlative with the concentration of ClO− and linearly in the range of 0.1–50 μmol·L–1 with the sensitivity of 0.03 μmol·L–1. The probe can also be further illustrated in a prospective application for determination of ClO− in environmental water through both solution response and filer paper sensing. Moreover, the positive biocompatibility and ignorable cytotoxicity made the probe a promising effective agent for detection and visualizing ClO− in living cells which can facilitate the understanding the oxidative stress from the overexpressing ClO−.

ce:section.ce:para: The homeostasis of the redox status in vivo provides crucial significances in physiological and pathological researches [1,2]. The oxidative damages, which are generally induced by the reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS), have been considered to be closely associated with multifarious human diseases such as inflammation, neurodegenerative disorders, cardiovascular diseases and cancer [3–6]. Amongst a variety of attractive ROS and RNS, hypochlorous acid or hypochlorite (HClO/ClO−) is one of the most important endogenous microbicide and high − efficiency oxidant which plays pivotal roles not only in diverse physiological processes but also in various pathological processes [7–9]. At normal concentrations, ClO− is beneficial to the repair of biological tissues and destroy of invasive bacteria and pathogens [10,11]. Unfortunately, excess content of ClO− leads to oxidative stress which subsequently caused tissue injuries and various disorders including arthritis, atherosclerosis, lung injury and kidney disease [12,13]. Moreover, ClO– has also been widely applied as a disinfectant in our daily life for the treatment of drinking water, swimming pool water as well as routine indoor and outdoor disinfection [14]. The residual concentration of free chlorine in these samples should be in the proper range and match the national standards to ensure safe water supply by sanitizing certain bacteria and bring no health hazards which are caused by excessive ClO– concentration. In light of the indispensable roles of ClO− in biological organs, processes as well as our daily life, the development of simple, rapid, sensitive, selective, and reliable ClO−−targeting detection strategies are of great importance and have received increasing attentions [15,16].

In comparison to traditional analytical methods such as iodometric titration, coulometry and polarography which not only need cumbersome operations but also suffer low sensitivity and long reaction time, fluorescence analysis with smart sensor enjoys the advantageous features of simplicity convenient operation, real–time speedy response, high sensitivity and selectivity as well as low cytotoxicity for visualizing the dynamic variation of ClO− in living cells, tissues, organs and bodies with excellent spatial and temporal resolution [2,4,5,15]. Consequently, a large number of fluorescent sensors have been reported for specific determination of ClO− in literature, especially for those based on small–molecule organic compounds which are in existing of superiority in favorable optical properties including long wavelength excitation and emission profiles, large extinction coefficient and high quantum yields [17–20]. However, the application of these probes are frequently influenced by poor water solubility and biocompatibility. Therefore, it is still the top priority to design the effective fluorescent probes to sense of ClO− in environmental and biological samples without the limitation of water solubility and biocompatibility.

In regard of the special structural and optical properties along with outstanding biocompatibility, carbon nanomaterial functionalized fluorescent probes have recently stood out and been widely applied in the areas of environmental, biological and medical sensing [21,22]. As the basic framework, carbon endows diversified structural variability and modifiability to this kind of nanomaterial from zero–dimension to three dimension including 0D carbon quantum dots (CDs), graphene quantum dot (GQDs), carbon nano onions (CNOs) and fullerenes, 1D carbon nano tubes (CNTs), carbon nanohorns, carbon nanofibers and carbon nanoribbons, 2D graphene and graphene oxides as well as 3D carbon nanodiamonds [23–25], which have attracted a great deal of attention in biological sensing since their excellent biological affinity, unique photoelectric properties and fluorescence performance, thus in achievement of accurate and efficient targeted detection and imaging [26–29]. The functionalization of the fluorescent probes with carbon nanomaterial can further provide outstanding water solubility and biocompatibility, low toxicity, stable photoluminescence characteristics, simple preparation from wide sources and widely modification with various functional groups, thereby make them popular and attractive in biological fluorescent sensing [30,31].

To date, various fluorescent probes based on carbon nanomaterial have been presented for identification and labeling of bioactive molecules such as ions, ROS and RNS, mercaptans, proteins/peptides, enzymes and nucleic acids, in which the 0D materials are quite popular especially probes based on the CDs [32–38]. CDs are 0D carbon–based particles in composed of amorphous or multilayer crystalline nuclei particles with a quasi–spherical structure, which have emerged as an exciting nanomaterial due to the absorbing flexibility, abundant easily modified and optimized surface as well as stably controllable fluorescence, making them potential candidates as selective fluorescent probes [39–43]. However, the CDs based fluorescent sensors are still limited by certain shortcomings such as low fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and poor fluorescent stability. To our knowledge, element doping, surface functionalization and material co–assembly are effective methods to promote the chemical and optical properties [26,35,36]. Among which, doping with a heteroatom, especially nonmetal elements including N, P, B, S, etc., is the most efficient, convenient and environmental friendly way to improve the fluorescent performance by adjusting internal electron configuration and providing active sites and functional groups thus ultimately enhancing the fluorescence quantum yield, luminescence efficiency and biocompatibility, which is highly demanded by biological tracking and imaging.

With these criteria in mind, herein we present a favorable N–doped CDs based fluorescent probe Y1 which was constructed via a one–step hydrothermal synthesis from polyvinyl pyrrolidone, L–arginine and tryptophan (Fig. 1 ) for selective responding of ClO− without the interference of the common co–existed bioactive molecules. Taking advantages of the splendid chemical and optical properties such as significant photostability, superior photobleaching resistance, high selective and interference immunity as well as excellent sensitivity, water solubility and biocompatibility, the probe has been successfully validated to be a promising powerful diagnostic tool for monitoring ClO− concentration and distribution in environmental systems and living cells.

The fluorescent spectra were measured on a Horiba FluoroMax–4 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Absorbance spectra were performed on a Hitachi U–2900 spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscope images were obtained from a JEM–1400 Flash analyzer. Raman spectra were performed with ThermoFisher DXR 2 spectroscopy. Fluorescent decays were conducted on an Edinburgh FLSP920 steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer. X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra were measured on a ULVAC–PHI PHI5000VersaProbeIII spectrophotometer with the excitation source of Al Kα. X–Ray powder diffraction spectroscopy analyses were performed on the Bruker D8 Advance analyzer. IR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer. Bioimaging of the sensors were performed on an Olympus FV1000 confocal microscopn and the excitation wavelength was set as 408 nm. The cell viability was determined at 405 nm absorbance using an ELx800 Absorbance Reader (BioTek Instruments, Inc.). NMR spectra were performed on a Varian Inova–400 MHz spectrometer (at 400 MHz for 1H) with tetramethylsilane (TMS) as internal standard. Xe lamp irradiation was conducted by the CEL–HXF300–T3 Xenon lamp source system with the light intensity of 1500 mW/cm2. The chemicals and reagents were all obtained from Inno–Chem Co. LLC.

Water samples: swimming pool water was from Xi’an University of Science and Technology, Lintong, Xi’ an, China; tap water was from Xi’an University of Science and Technology, Lintong, Xi’ an, China; purified water was from a water purifier, UPH–I–60L, Chengdu, China; 84 disinfectant samples was from Langjie 84 disinfectant, Huyi, Xi’ an, China. The 84 disinfectant samples were used diluted for 5.0 × 10–4 equiv. before using.

All experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines of China and have been approved by Xi’an University of Science and Technology (Xi’an, China) and Northwest University (Xi’an, China). No experimentation with human subjects were included in this work. The Human Osteosarcoma MG–63 cells and Mouse fibroblast cells L929 were purchased from the College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Northwest University (Xi’an, China).

The probe is synthesized by one–pot hydrothermal method. In short, 0.5 g of polyvinylpyrrolidone, 0.5 g of L–arginine and 0.5 g of tryptophan were added to a volume of 50 mL of double–distilled water to form homogeneous solution, after all the solids were completely dissolved, the above solution was uniformly transferred to three Teflon–lined stainless steel vessel (50 mL) and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the final product was collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm,10 min) to remove large particles, and then dialyzed against ultrapure water (5000, molecular weight cutoff) for 24 h to remove unreacted reagents. The final product was freeze–dried under vacuum to obtain a solid sample, which is stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use.

Stock solutions of the probe (50 mg·L–1) and ions (500 μmol·L–1) were all prepared in distilled water. The solutions of the ions were prepared with AgNO3, CaC12, PbC12, MgCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·5H2O, CdCl2·2.5H2O, ZnCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, CoC12·6H2O, NiC12·6H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, SnCl2, HgC12, anhydrous FeCl3 and AlC13, SnCl4, NaNO3, Na3PO4, Na2SO3, Na2S, Na2C2O4, Na2CO3, NaOAc, NaClO4, NaClO3, NaClO2, NaClO, Cys (cysteine), Hcy (homocysteine), GSH (glutathione), ATP (adenosine triphosphate), ADP (adenosine diphosphate), AMP (adenosine monophosphate), DA (dopamine), AA (ascorbic acid) as well as the ROS and RNS (H2O2, O2 ·–, NO, ONOO–) which were obtained follow the reported procedure and all prepared in 500 μmol·L–1. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental method of filter paper sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The filter paper was cut into rectangle shapes and put into the solution of the probe. After soaking for 1 h. the prepared solution (100 μmol·L–1) of the interested ions including: Ag+, Sn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Sn4+, AMP, ADP, ATP, Cys, GSH, Hcy, O2 ·–, H2O2, NO, S2–, ONOO–, NO3 2–, PO4 3–, CO3 2–, OAc–, C2O4 2–, SO3 2–, AA, DA, ClO4 –, ClO3 –, ClO2 –, ClO– were added and soaked for another hour, respectively.

Another group of the filter papers were soaked with the practical water samples of swimming pool water, tap water, distilled water, purified water and 84 disinfectant samples for 1 h after soaking with the probe for 1 h. Then the papers were brought out and taken photos under the portable UV lamp with excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

In vitro cell viability assay: MCF−7 (1 × 106 to 5 × 106 cells) were seeded in 96−well plates and incubated in 150 μL of medium (RPMI Medium Modified, HyClone with 10% gibco FBS (Art. No. 10270) and 1% hydrostreptomycin (P/S), HyClone) added into each plate at 37 °C in the atmosphere of 5% CO2 to allow the cells to attach. After incubated for 24 h, the medium was sucked out and 100 μL of fresh medium was added. A stock solution of 5 mg·L–1 of the probe was prepared in distilled water and stored at −20 °C. The stock solution was diluted to appropriate concentrations with cultured medium. The solution of the probe in the cultured medium were serial diluted with final concentration of 0, 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 mg·mL−1 and added to the microtiter plate and incubated for 24 h in the atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 °C and then was sucked out the medium.

Fluorescent imaging in living cells: Fluorescent imaging in living Human Osteosarcoma MG−Figs. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 7 63 cells and Mouse fibroblast cells L929 were performed in the similar procedures. The cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% FBS, at 37 °C in the humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 and 95% air. The cells were then cultured for 4 h until they plated on glass–bottomed dishes. The growth medium was then removed and the cells were washed with DMEM without FBS and imaged. The cells were then incubated with 1 mg·L–1 of the probe for 30 min at 37 °C, washed three times with PBS and imaged. Then the cells were supplemented with 50 μmol·L–1 ClO– in the growth medium for 30 min at 37 °C and imaged.

Probe Y1 is facilely prepared through one–step hydrothermal process using L–arginine, polyvinyl pyrrolidone and tryptophan as the carbon precursor and subsequently fully characterized to highlight the morphology, microstructure and physicochemical properties. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images clearly indicated the spherical–like shape of the probe which were well–dispersed (Fig. 2 a, 2b) [44]. HRTEM images clearly resolved lattice parameter of 0.19 nm which was corresponded to the basic spacing of graphite [45] (Fig. 2c) and showed various crystalline structures with the diameters of 1.17–3.16 nm in which most of the CDs diameters varied from 2 nm to 3 nm with the average diameter of 2.45 nm (Fig. 2d). The crystallinity was also supported by the X–Ray powder diffraction (XRD) in which an obvious broad peak (Fig. 2e) appeared around 26° (2θ) which was attributed to the (002) plane of graphene structure of carbon [46,47]. The Raman spectrum exhibited two peaks at 1300 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 (Fig. 2f), which belong to the disordered structures (D band) and graphite structures (G band) of carbon materials, respectively. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was calculated to be 0.18, suggesting the containing of sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbonaceous cores with more graphitization structures [48,49].

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR) spectrum was then constructed to gain an insight of the functional moieties on the surface of Y1 (Fig. 3 a) [50]. The broad absorption band around 3445 cm−1 confirmed the existence of OH group. The sharp peak at 2890 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CH and the peak at 1465 cm−1 was assigned to the CH bending vibrations. The peaks at 1716 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1 were the typical landmark of the CO and CN groups, respectively, thus provided evidence for the possession of COOH group. The peak at 1342 cm−1 was mostly probably assigned to the deformation vibration of the phenolic OH moiety. The peak at 1114 cm−1 could be ascribed to the –CO stretching, while the deformation vibration of OH⋯O was corresponded to be 951 cm−1, which provided another evidence for the containing of COOH in the CDs. We further investigated the chemical identity and functional groups by X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) [51]. The peaks of C 1 s (285.65 eV), N 1 s (531.94 eV), and O 1 s (400.00 eV) were found (Fig. 3c), indicating that the fluorescence probes were composed of the elements of C, N, and O. In high–resolution spectrum, the C 1 s band was fitted into four distinct carbon states of CC at 284.8 eV, CO / CN at 286.64 eV, and CO at 288.06 eV, respectively, demonstrating the presence of sp2 carbon (Fig. 3d). The N 1 s spectrum was split into three peaks centered at 398.44 eV, 399.73 eV and 401.27 eV, which were attributed to pyridinic N, amide N, and amine N (Fig. 3e). In addition, the O 1 s peaks were mainly concentrated at 531.13 eV and 532.64 eV, which were consistent with CO and CO (Fig. 3f). Both the FT–IR and XPS results illustrated the successfully doping of nitrogen into the framework of Y1 as well as the existence of abundant nitrogen and oxygen containing functional groups including OH and COOH which may increase the active sites on the surface of Y1, thus ensuring good biocompatibility and solubility.

The UV−vis absorption, the fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of the asprepared probe Y1 are shown in Fig. 4 a. The UV−vis spectrum exhibited a characteristic maximum absorption peak located around 349 nm, which was attributed to the n − π* transition of CO and CN bonds. Meanwhile, the fluorescent excitation spectrum displayed that the maximum excitation wavelength was manifested as 350 nm. Furthermore, a distinct emission peak appeared at 439 nm which exhibited bright blue fluorescence in both solid state and solution state when irradiated by a UV light at 365 nm with a large Stokes shift of about 90 nm (Fig. 4a, inset). In addition, it is also noticed that the maximum fluorescence emission of probe Y1 displayed red shifts with increasing excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm, in which the emission fluorescence intensity at 439 nm increase when the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 350 nm while fluorescence intensity decrease were also recorded under the excitation wavelength from 350 to 400 nm, suggesting the optimal excitation wavelength of 350 nm in fluorescent analysis (Fig. 4b). The fluorescent lifetime decay of probe Y1 was studied with excitation at 368 nm [52], in which the decay curves were optimum–fitted to a biexponential functions in average lifetime of 2.79 ns from the decay time τ1 4.31 ns (44%) and τ2 1.59 ns (56%) with χ2 value of 1.157 (Fig. 3b). The fluorescent quantum yield of Y1 was calculated to be 20.20% following the reported procedure by using quinine sulfate in a 0.1 mol·L–1 H2SO4 solution (quantum yield 54%) as a reference [53].

The optical stability has been investigated under various conditions including effects of pH, ionic strength and UV exposure before going application. It is clear that the fluorescence intensity of probe Y1 kept almostly constant with increasing concentration of NaCl in which no significant emission changes were found even in the high ionic strength of 1.0 mol·L–1 (Fig. S1), indicating the excellent stability of Y1 in high sodium ion environment. The significant photostability of Y1 was supported by the facts that the fluorescence intensity of Y1 exhibited favorable resistance to photobleaching after 90 days (Fig. S2) of storage at room temperature and direct continuous UV and Xe irradiation for 120 min, as illustrated in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of probe demonstrated quietly stable in the pH range of 1 to 12.0 and insignificantly decrease only in the strongly basic condition of pH 13 (Fig. 4d). In addition, neither the high concentration of H2O2 nor GSH could induce significant changes of the fluorescent intensity of Y1 (Fig. S5, S6). The above results provided strong evidences for the prominently optical stability of Y1 in different conditions which guarantee the successfully application of Y1 for selective fluorescence sensing in complicated biological and environmental conditions.

The capability of probe Y1 as a nanosensor for selective determination was investigated. For a promising probe, high specificity is one of the necessity, thus the selective detection was firstly carried out by adding 50 μmol·L–1 concentration of various interested ions including: Ag+, Sn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Sn4+, Cys, Hcy, GSH, ATP, ADP, AMP, AA, DA, O2 ·–, H2O2, NO, S2–, ONOO–, NO3 2–, PO4 3–, CO3 2–, OAc–, C2O4 2–, SO3 2–, ClO4 –, ClO3 –, ClO2 –, ClO– to the solution with specific 1 mg·L–1 amount of Y1 in the same condition. As demonstrated in Fig. 4c, the addition of metal ion as Pb2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ caused very slight fluorescence quenching, which was significantly inferior and negligible compared with the remarkable quenching induced by ClO–, clearly confirmed that Y1 was highly specific and sensitive toward ClO–. To further explore the utility of the probe, competition experiments were subsequently performed. As presented in Fig. 4c, no significant changes in the fluorescence intensity were observed in the presence of other commonly coexistent substances in the specific sensing process of ClO–, except for the slight increases caused by NO, S2–, SO3 2– and AA, respectively.

Before adapt the probe to ClO– detection in practical samples, some experimental conditions such as the reaction time, pH condition, sensing range and the limit of detection (LOD) should be optimized. The effects of pH on the sensing platform were firstly studied. The fluorescence intensity of the probe remained in a wide range of stability performance within the studied pH range of 4.0 to 8.0 (Fig. 4d), which not only indicates that the probe could detect ClO– in the neutral pH range with excellent stability, but also lays strong solid foundation for future biological applications. The response time manifested that the reaction of Y1 with ClO– could be finished within 10 min (Fig. S8). As a result, to obtain precise sensing results, 10 min was chosen as the ideal reaction time to ensure complete reaction between Y1 and ClO– before detection. With the optimized reaction time and pH condition, fluorescent titration were then carried out by gradually addition of ClO– into the solution of Y1. Fig. 4e shed light on the fact that emission intensity of Y1 underwent concentration corresponded decreasing which was proportional to ClO– concentration in the range from 0.1 to 50 μmol·L–1 (Fig. 4f) in good linearity (R 2 = 0.9979) with the LOD calculated to be 0.03 μmol·L–1 according to the Kaiser’s definition, which enjoyed the superior sensitivity and linear range to the recently reported ClO– probes based on CDs and organic small molecule fluorophores [31,54–60]. The results were apparent that both the wide linear range and the low LOD demonstrated a better prospect of application of the probe which was sensitive enough to ensure quantitative analysis of ClO– and free chlorine in related practical environmental and biological water samples.

In order to verify the quenching mechanism, UV–vis absorption, FT–IR spectra, 1H NMR spectra, fluorescence lifetime and XPS were further measured. Fig. 5 a described the UV−vis absorption spectra of Y1 and Y1–ClO–. Compared with the obvious UV−vis absorption of Y1 at 349 nm, it is clear that no absorption peak was found in Y1-ClO– around 349 nm. In FT–IR spectra, the peaks at 1342 cm−1 and 1114 cm−1 which were assigned to the deformation vibration of the phenolic OH moiety and the CO stretching, respectively, almost completely disappeared, while the peak at 1716 cm−1 enhanced significantly (Fig. 5b), demonstrating that the phenolic groups in Y1 were oxidized [52]. Meanwhile, the 1H NMR of Y1–ClO– showed that the peaks between 6 ppm and 8 ppm were significantly reduced (Fig. 5c), which was most possibly due to the change of benzene ring structure to the quinone structure [52,61]. Furthermore, the decrease of fluorescence lifetime (0.62 ns) from 2.79 ns to 2.17 ns (Fig. 5d) also provided evidence for the intramolecular charge transfer of Y1 in addition of ClO– [62]. In addition, the XPS spectra illustrated that the addition of ClO– decreased the percentage of N element and significant increased the percentage of O and Cl elements in Y1–ClO– (Fig. 5e, 5 g, 5 h, 5i) [52,63,64]. Moreover, The N 1 s spectrum indicated that the peak at 401.37 which corresponded to the amine bond disappeared in the N 1 s spectrum of Y1–ClO– (Fig. 5g), which may attributed to the oxidation process of the amino to the quinone structure upon addition of ClO–. On the basis of the above results, the quenching mechanism of Y1 upon addition of ClO– could be attributed to the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) processes in which the oxidability of ClO– oxidized the amino and hydroxyl groups to the quinone structure that resulted in the fluorescent quenching of Y1, as depict schematically in Fig. 5j.

The probe was then used for detecting ClO– in practical environmental water samples including the swimming pool water, tap water, distilled water, purified water and commercial 84 disinfectant samples (Table S1). The fluorescence intensity of the Y1 solution decreased obvious in addition of the swimming pool water, tap water and commercial 84 disinfectant sample which contained ClO– and meanwhile distilled water and purified water induced no fluorescence quenching since none ClO– was exist in these samples. The average concentration of ClO– were calculated to be 14.3113 μmol·L–1 (0.7375 mg·L–1) in the tap water, 7.2888 μmol·L–1 (0.3756 mg·L–1) in the swimming pool water, which met the national “Drink Water Quality Standards” (GB 5749–2006) and “Swimming Hygiene Standards” (GB 9776–1996) of China, respectively. In addition, the concentration of ClO– in the commercial 84 disinfectant sample was recorded as 1.0909 mol·L–1 (56.2138 mg·L–1, 5.6109%), in which the residual concentration of free chlorine was in accordance with the national standards and the label concentration of 4%–7% on the bottle. Compared with the national standards (GB/T 11898–1989, 5750.11–2006) for detection of free chlorine, total chlorine and disinfectants parameters of water quality and drinking water which not only need tedious test procedures but also limited by the low sensitivity of 0.01 mg·L–1 (0.196 μmol·L–1) and interfering materials such as Fe3+ and nitrite, detection of ClO– by Y1 is regarded to be more simple, stable, sensitive and effective. On the basis of these detection results, the Y1 exhibited promising potential as an efficient tool for qualitative and quantitative determination of ClO– containing environmental water samples.

With the excellent optical properties in mind, we explore the possibility for using Y1 as a filter paper sensor. The filter papers were soaked in the solution of the probe for 1 h and the prepared solution of the interested ions for another hour. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of the filter papers showed obvious fluorescence quenching only after treated with the solution containing ClO–, which was highly consistent with the fluorescence selectivity investigation. In addition, the fluorescence of the filter papers displayed weak fluorescent decreasing when soaked with swimming pool water and tap water, while distinct fluorescent quenching were found in the paper which were treated with the commercial 84 disinfectant sample, exhibited significant agreement with test results in the solution condition. This result indicated that Y1 could be applied as a filter paper sensor for specifically rapid qualitative identifying of ClO– in practical complex environmental samples.

The fluorescence pictures of Human Osteosarcoma MG–63 cells and Mouse fibroblast cells L929 were attained on a laser confocal microscope. In accordance with Fig. 7 , the cells were nonfluorescent in the bright field, after incubated with Y1 for 30 min at 37 °C, bright blue fluorescence were observed in the cells. In addition, as evident from the overlay diagram, the bright fluorescent emission mainly located in cytoplasmand especially cocooned in the nucleus of cells in which the nanometer–sized Y1 were completely internalized into the cells and the cells were capable of maintaining a good morphology, which was consistent with the MTT results of excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity of Y1 (Fig. S9). When 50 μmol·L–1 of ClO– was supplemented into the growth medium for another 30 min at 37 °C, the microscopy image showed very weak intracellular fluorescent emission. Bright–field transmission images of cells treated with the probe and ClO– confirmed that the cells were viable throughout the imaging experiments. The bioimaging results revealed that Y1 was nontoxic and stable in biological systems, which signified an effective fluorescent nanoprobe for cell imaging and monitoring of ClO– in living cells.

To summarize, a novel probe Y1 based on fluorescent CDs was fabricated from a low–cost and facile one–step hydrothermal methodology from polyvinyl pyrrolidone, L–arginine and tryptophan for sensing of ClO– in which the quenching phenomenon of fluorescence was occurred induced by ClO–. The probe exhibits outstanding optical properties such as high brightness, good quantum yield, excellent photobleaching resistance from different pH values, ionic strength impact and ultraviolet or xenon irradiation as well as brilliant water solubility and biocompatibility which are highly suitable for utilizing as a fascinating tool to achieve rapid response to ClO– with prominent selectivity, sensitivity and inert to large number of possible interfering bioactive substances with satisfactory results. These properties can not only contribute to potential application for quantitative detection of ClO– in real environmental water samples and qualitative sensing by the filter paper coated with Y1, but also manifest a favorable bioimaging capability for sensing of ClO– in living cells, thus providing significant insight into a better understanding of the importance of ClO– in related biological samples and processes.

Zheng Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Tiantian Xu: Resources, Investigation. Xu Zhang: Investigation. Hui Li: Investigation, Data curation. Xiaodan Jia: Formal analysis, Validation. Shunsheng Zhao: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zaiwen Yang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Xiangrong Liu: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Prof. Dr. Jianli Li and Dr. Mengyao She of Northwest University for their great help in structural characterization and cell imaging experiments.
